content.text: Governance without a schedule is intention without execution. For data stewards managing AI and GenAI assets, the review cadence determines when known risks are examined, when quality trends are spotted before they become incidents, and when compliance attestations are renewed. A cadence that is too infrequent misses fast-moving quality degradation in RAG corpora; one that is too frequent burns steward time without proportionate benefit. The right cadence is tuned to the rate of change of the data asset and the severity of consequences from quality failures.

For AI systems, the stakes differ from traditional data warehousing: a stale RAG corpus does not produce a missing row in a report — it produces a confident AI response grounded in outdated facts, delivered at scale to end users. The review cadence for AI data assets must be calibrated to this higher consequence profile.
Why It Matters

Review cadences serve three distinct purposes. First, they create a predictable schedule that stewards can plan around, converting an abstract governance obligation into a concrete calendar commitment. Second, they establish a baseline of continuous attention that detects drift before it becomes an incident — a monthly review of a RAG corpus freshness metric will catch a broken ingestion pipeline in weeks, not months. Third, they create an audit trail of continuous oversight that satisfies regulatory requirements for ongoing data governance.

Without a defined cadence, reviews happen reactively — triggered only by incidents. This is the worst pattern: the review happens after the harm has occurred. A cadence-driven model catches quality issues before they affect model outputs and before they produce regulatory exposure.
Benefits
Predictability: stewards know when reviews are due; calendar integration prevents scheduling conflicts.
Trend detection: regular measurement reveals slow-moving degradation (e.g., gradual freshness decay) that point-in-time checks miss.
Audit evidence: completed reviews with timestamps and findings provide documentary evidence of ongoing governance.
Forcing function: scheduled reviews create accountability — a missed review is a visible governance gap, not an invisible one.
Escalation integration: the cadence defines the normal path; events that require immediate action are escalated outside the cadence.
Trade-offs
Time cost: reviews that are too frequent consume steward time without proportionate risk reduction — weekly reviews for assets that change monthly add overhead without benefit.
Alert fatigue: if every automated check generates a review task, stewards tune out; the cadence must filter signal from noise.
Static cadence risk: a fixed monthly cadence may be appropriate today but wrong after a major system change; cadences need their own review cycle.
Review Cadence Tiers

attrs.alt: Governance calendar showing weekly automated checks, monthly domain steward reviews, quarterly formal governance reviews, and event-triggered reviews outside the normal cadence

content.text: Continuous (Automated)

Automated quality checks run continuously or on short intervals (hourly, daily). These are not human reviews — they are monitoring pipelines that collect metrics: freshness delta, null rates, row counts, retrieval precision on synthetic queries. When a metric breaches a threshold, an alert is raised. Stewards review alerts, not every metric datapoint. The continuous tier is infrastructure, not governance — it feeds the human review tiers.
Weekly

The AI/ML Steward or Technical Steward reviews the automated dashboard for anomalies, reviews open alerts, and confirms that ingestion pipelines ran successfully. This review is typically 30-60 minutes and focuses on operations: nothing new should require escalation from a healthy system. If the weekly review finds nothing, that itself is recorded as evidence of continuous oversight.
Monthly

The Domain Data Steward conducts a structured review covering: quality metric trends for the month, any source documents added or removed from the corpus, consent and classification status of new additions, open remediation tickets and their resolution status, and a sign-off on the asset's fitness for continued AI use. The monthly review is the primary governance cadence for most AI data assets.
Quarterly

The formal governance review involves the CDO, all domain stewards, and representatives from Legal and Compliance. It reviews: year-to-date quality trend data, steward performance against SLAs, cross-domain issues and resolutions, updates to AI data governance policy, and any new regulatory requirements affecting the data assets. The quarterly review produces a formal governance report.
Mental Model: Medical Checkup Schedule

Think of review cadences like a preventive healthcare schedule. A blood pressure machine (automated monitoring) runs daily. A family doctor visit (monthly steward review) checks trends and catches early problems. A specialist consultation (quarterly governance review) reviews the full picture and adjusts long-term treatment. Emergency room visits (event-triggered reviews) handle acute problems outside the normal schedule. No patient relies on emergency room visits as their primary healthcare — and no AI program should rely on incidents as its primary governance trigger.
Implementing Review Cadences

# Governance calendar management for AI data assets
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Callable
from datetime import date, datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class ReviewType(Enum):
    AUTOMATED = "automated"
    WEEKLY = "weekly"
    MONTHLY = "monthly"
    QUARTERLY = "quarterly"
    EVENT_TRIGGERED = "event_triggered"

class ReviewStatus(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    OVERDUE = "overdue"
    ESCALATED = "escalated"

@dataclass
class ReviewTask:
    task_id: str
    asset_id: str
    review_type: ReviewType
    assignee_email: str
    due_date: date
    status: ReviewStatus = ReviewStatus.PENDING
    completed_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    findings: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    escalated_to: Optional[str] = None

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return self.status == ReviewStatus.PENDING and date.today() > self.due_date

    def complete(self, findings: List[str]) -> None:
        self.status = ReviewStatus.COMPLETED
        self.completed_at = datetime.utcnow()
        self.findings = findings

class GovernanceCalendar:
    def __init__(self, asset_id: str):
        self.asset_id = asset_id
        self.tasks: List[ReviewTask] = []
        self._task_counter = 0

    def _next_id(self) -> str:
        self._task_counter += 1
        return f"REV-{self.asset_id}-{self._task_counter:04d}"

    def schedule_monthly_reviews(
        self,
        steward_email: str,
        start_date: date,
        months: int = 12
    ) -> List[ReviewTask]:
        tasks = []
        current = start_date
        for _ in range(months):
            task = ReviewTask(
                task_id=self._next_id(),
                asset_id=self.asset_id,
                review_type=ReviewType.MONTHLY,
                assignee_email=steward_email,
                due_date=current
            )
            self.tasks.append(task)
            tasks.append(task)
            # Advance to same day next month
            if current.month == 12:
                current = current.replace(year=current.year + 1, month=1)
            else:
                current = current.replace(month=current.month + 1)
        return tasks

    def get_overdue(self) -> List[ReviewTask]:
        return [t for t in self.tasks if t.is_overdue()]

    def get_upcoming(self, days: int = 7) -> List[ReviewTask]:
        cutoff = date.today() + timedelta(days=days)
        return [
            t for t in self.tasks
            if t.status == ReviewStatus.PENDING and t.due_date <= cutoff
        ]

# Example usage
cal = GovernanceCalendar("cust-kb-v2")
monthly_tasks = cal.schedule_monthly_reviews(
    steward_email="jane.smith@example.com",
    start_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
    months=12
)
print(f"Scheduled {len(monthly_tasks)} monthly reviews")
overdue = cal.get_overdue()
print(f"Overdue reviews: {len(overdue)}")
Event-Triggered Reviews
Scheduled reviews catch gradual drift. Event-triggered reviews catch acute changes. Every AI governance program must define which events automatically initiate an out-of-cadence steward review:
Model update: when the foundation model or embedding model is upgraded, the steward reviews compatibility of existing data assets with the new model.
Quality SLA breach: any automated alert indicating a metric has breached its defined threshold triggers an immediate review within 4 hours for Tier 1 assets.
New data source onboarding: before a new document source is added to a RAG corpus, the steward conducts a classification, consent, and quality review.
Security incident: any incident involving the data asset (unauthorized access, data leak) triggers an immediate steward review and incident report.
Regulatory change: publication of new regulations or guidance affecting the data asset triggers a review of compliance posture within 5 business days.
Steward succession: when a steward leaves or changes roles, a review is triggered to transfer knowledge and re-validate the asset's governance status.
Review Checklist Template

# Monthly steward review checklist for a RAG corpus
review:
  asset_id: cust-kb-v2
  review_type: monthly
  period: 2026-07
  steward: jane.smith@example.com
  
quality_metrics:
  freshness_lag_avg_hours: 3.2      # target: <24h
  freshness_lag_max_hours: 18.5     # target: <48h
  null_rate_critical_fields: 0.001  # target: <0.01
  retrieval_precision_at_5: 0.847   # target: >0.80
  coverage_score: 0.923             # target: >0.90
  
changes_this_month:
  documents_added: 145
  documents_removed: 23
  sources_added: 0
  sources_removed: 0
  embedding_model_changed: false
  
open_issues:
  - ticket: DQ-2341
    severity: medium
    description: "3 product FAQ pages have broken source links"
    status: in-progress
    owner: bob.lee@example.com
    
consent_and_classification:
  all_documents_classified: true
  consent_verified_for_new_additions: true
  pii_documents_flagged: 2
  pii_review_complete: true
  
steward_decision:
  fit_for_use: true
  issues_requiring_escalation: false
  notes: "Retrieval precision improved from 0.831 to 0.847 after chunking adjustment"
  signed_off_at: 2026-07-31T14:23:00Z
Real-World Example

A legal technology company with a contract analysis GenAI system established a tiered review cadence after a quality incident: a new jurisdiction's contract law documents were added to the RAG corpus without a proper domain steward review, causing the system to give inconsistent answers about contract enforceability in that jurisdiction for six weeks before the issue was discovered through a user complaint.

The company implemented: daily automated freshness checks, weekly AI/ML Steward pipeline reviews, monthly Legal Domain Steward document quality reviews (with a mandatory checklist), and quarterly CDO governance reviews. They also added an event trigger: any document from a new jurisdiction requires a Legal Domain Steward review before indexing. Within 90 days of implementing the cadence, they detected and remediated two quality issues during monthly reviews — neither reached production.
Common Mistakes

Setting all assets to the same review cadence regardless of tier is the most common mistake. A fine-tuning dataset that is updated annually does not need monthly reviews; a RAG corpus that is updated daily does. Cadence must be calibrated to the asset's rate of change and consequence of quality failure. The governance overhead of over-reviewing low-change assets burns steward time that could address higher-risk assets.

A second mistake is treating the review as a checkbox exercise rather than a quality conversation. If the monthly review consists only of signing a form without examining the actual metrics, it provides audit trail but not governance. The review checklist must require the steward to interpret the metrics, not just attest that they were collected.
Best Practices
Calibrate cadence to asset tier: Tier 1 (production RAG corpora) monthly minimum; Tier 3 (archived training sets) quarterly.
Integrate review tasks with the steward's calendar system (Outlook, Google Calendar) with reminders 5 days before due date.
Use structured checklists, not free-form notes — checklists create comparable records across review periods.
Record every review outcome in the data catalog: findings, decisions, and the steward's sign-off with timestamp.
Separate automated monitoring from human review — the automated layer feeds the human layer, it does not replace it.
Define escalation SLAs: a Tier 1 quality breach should trigger steward action within 4 hours, not 30 days.
Performance and Scalability

A steward managing 20 AI data assets cannot conduct a deep review of each asset every month. Scalable cadence design uses risk-based scheduling: higher-risk assets get more frequent reviews. Risk is a function of: production impact (does it ground a customer-facing AI?), rate of change (how often does the source data update?), and regulatory sensitivity (does it contain PII or regulated content?). A risk scoring model maps each asset to a cadence tier automatically as asset metadata changes.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Collibra Workflow: configures scheduled review tasks with steward assignment and deadline tracking.
Jira / ServiceNow: creates review tickets on a schedule with checklist templates and SLA enforcement.
Apache Airflow: orchestrates automated data quality checks that feed the review dashboard.
Monte Carlo / Acceldata: data observability platforms that generate automated quality scores for steward dashboards.
PagerDuty: routes event-triggered review alerts to the on-call steward based on asset tier.

Security and Reliability

Review records must be tamper-evident. If a steward completes a review and later someone modifies the review record to change the outcome, the integrity of the governance process is compromised. Store review records in an append-only system (audit log, immutable storage, or a blockchain-backed catalog if regulatory requirements demand it). Each review record should be cryptographically signed by the steward to prevent repudiation.

For operational reliability, the governance calendar system must be monitored as a production service. If the system that generates review tasks is unavailable, stewards may miss scheduled reviews with no visibility. Implement alerts for review task generation failures and maintain a manual backup process (email reminders) as a fallback.
Key Takeaways
Review cadence converts governance intent into scheduled, accountable actions — without it, reviews only happen after incidents.
AI data assets typically require four cadence tiers: continuous automated monitoring, weekly operations check, monthly domain steward review, and quarterly governance review.
Event-triggered reviews handle acute changes outside the normal schedule: model updates, security incidents, new source onboarding, regulatory changes.
Cadence must be calibrated to asset tier: production RAG corpora need more frequent reviews than archived training sets.
Checklists with structured metrics make reviews auditable and comparable across periods — free-form notes are not sufficient.
Review records must be tamper-evident and signed by the steward for regulatory defensibility.
Scale cadence management with risk scoring: automate the assignment of assets to cadence tiers as their metadata changes.
